question: Will a Turkish civil court (Sulh Hukuk Mahkemesi) appoint a "call committee" (çağrı heyeti) to convene a CHP extraordinary congress before July 12, 2026?

factors.description: Status quo: no application confirmed filed and no ruling as of June 30; resolves NO if court hasn't ruled by July 11
Tight timeline: applications typically ~15 days; if filed June 29-30, median ruling ~July 14, after the window
Likely bilirkişi signature-examination step first (disputed 833 vs 820), which fine print excludes and which adds delay before any appointment
Strong deadline-driven urgency for expedited handling plus clear precedents (MHP 2016, CHP İstanbul) give court a model
Court may defer/postpone given politically explosive nature and pending 'absolute nullity' litigation

summary: As of June 30, 2026, the Sulh Hukuk Mahkemesi application was announced but not confirmed filed, and no ruling had been issued. For YES, three hurdles must clear within ~12 days: (1) the application is filed, (2) the court rules before July 11, and (3) the ruling is a favorable national-level çağrı heyeti appointment (not a rejection, postponement, or interim bilirkişi-only step). Applications 'typically' take ~15 days, which would push the median ruling past the July 11 window, and the disputed signature count (833 vs 820) likely triggers a bilirkişi examination first — explicitly excluded by the fine print. Counterbalancing: Özel's camp faces a hard deadline (15-day notice for a July 26 congress) creating strong urgency for expedited handling, with clear precedents (MHP 2016, CHP İstanbul). The modal outcome remains 'court has not yet ruled by July 11.' Conditional chain ≈ 0.90 × 0.40 × 0.60 ≈ 0.22.
reasoning:
**Forecast: 22% (NO favored)**

The question asks whether a Turkish civil court will appoint a national-level çağrı heyeti for CHP between June 27 – July 11, 2026. As of June 30, the application wasn't even confirmed filed and no ruling had been issued. Three hurdles must all clear within ~12 days: filing, a ruling before July 11, and a *favorable appointment* (not rejection/postponement/bilirkişi-only). The typical ~15-day processing time would push the median ruling past the window, and the disputed signature count likely triggers an expert examination first (explicitly excluded by fine print). Strong deadline-driven urgency and clear precedents provide upward pressure, but the modal outcome remains "court has not yet ruled by July 11" — yielding **0.22**.
